item.title: Berry Gordy
item.description: Born: November 28, 1929 (Detroit, Michigan) Active: 1957–present (label-building era: 1959–1988) Role: Label founder, songwriter, producer Key institutions: Motown Records (1959–1988), Jobete Music (publishing), Motown Productions (film/television) Wikipedia · RYM The $800 that founded Tamla Records...

item.title: Chess Records
item.description: Founded: 1950 (as Chess Records; predecessor Aristocrat Records from 1947) Location: Chicago, Illinois (2120 South Michigan Avenue, 1957–1967) Founders: Leonard Chess, Phil Chess Key years: 1950–1969 Subsidiaries: Checker Records (1952), Argo Records (1955; renamed Cadet, 1965) RYM The sound of 2120...

item.title: Philles Records
item.description: Founded: 1961 Location: New York City / Los Angeles Founders: Phil Spector and Lester Sill Key years: 1962–1966 Key genres: Girl group, Pop soul RYM The drums on “Be My Baby” hit like a door swinging open into a room with no back wall.

item.title: Sun Records
item.description: Founded: February 1, 1952 (predecessor Memphis Recording Service, January 3, 1950) Location: Memphis, Tennessee (706 Union Avenue) Founder: Sam Phillips Key years: 1952–1968 (sold to Shelby Singleton, July 1969) Subsidiaries: Flip Records (1955), Phillips International Records (October 1957) RYM The...

item.title: Abbey Road Studios
item.description: Location: 3 Abbey Road, St John’s Wood, London Years of peak activity: 1931–present (as EMI Studios until 1970, Abbey Road Studios thereafter) Key engineers: Norman Smith, Geoff Emerick, Ken Townsend, Alan Parsons EMI opened Abbey Road Studios in 1931 in a converted Georgian townhouse, and for three...

item.title: Gold Star Studios
item.description: Location: 6252 Santa Monica Boulevard, Hollywood, California Years of peak activity: 1950s–1984 (demolished 1984) Key engineers: Larry Levine, Stan Ross, Dave Gold Gold Star’s echo chambers were a pair of trapezoidal concrete rooms built directly behind Studio A, and their acoustic properties gave r...

item.title: Wall of Sound
item.description: Origin: Phil Spector, Gold Star Studios, Hollywood, California, c.

item.title: Backbeat
item.description: Also known as: the backbeat, snare on two and four First heard in: African American gospel and blues of the early twentieth century; codified as the defining rhythmic feature of popular music on 1940s–50s New Orleans R&B recordings by drummer Earl Palmer A Motown record comes on the radio and wi...

item.title: I–vi–IV–V progression
item.description: Also known as: doo-wop changes, the ’50s progression, the Heart and Soul changes, ice cream changes First heard in: African American vocal group records of the early 1950s; codified as a pop song structure on girl group and Brill Building singles of the early 1960s, then absorbed into Beatles-era Br...
